summary: Gruber reports that Beeper Mini has returned after Apple blocked it, but now requires Apple ID sign-in instead of seamless phone number registration. He notes Beeper's decision to make the service free was responsible given the instability, but questions whether Android users actually want iMessage access. He dissects Beeper's argument that Messages being the default chat app justifies their approach, calling it disingenuous since users worldwide successfully ignore Messages for WhatsApp and Line. Gruber argues Beeper is essentially claiming entitlement to Apple's iMessage infrastructure. He also pushes back on Beeper's security claims, noting that Apple can guarantee security across protocol, client, OS, and hardware, while Beeper introduces variables Apple cannot control. He concludes that while the cat-and-mouse game is entertaining to watch, Beeper is in an unenviable position.
key_points: Beeper Mini is back but requires Apple ID sign-in rather than the seamless phone-number-based iMessage registration that originally made it compelling
Beeper made the service free, which Gruber considers responsible given they cannot guarantee continued access against Apple's countermeasures
Gruber calls Beeper's argument about Messages being the default chat app disingenuous, pointing out that users in WhatsApp-dominated countries have no problem ignoring Messages
Apple's security guarantee extends across protocol, client software, OS, and hardware — Beeper's existence breaks that chain of trust even if the protocol itself remains secure
The unauthorized client creates a perception problem for Apple: if they can't control who connects to iMessage, they've lost control of the platform
money_quotes: That's quite the euphemistic description of the situation.
It was irresponsible to charge a subscription fee for a service they can't guarantee access to.

If Android SMS users were interested in installing a third-party app to enable better cross-platform messaging, wouldn't they be suggesting to their iPhone-using friends and family that they be the ones who install WhatsApp or Signal or something?
People don't start new chats in the Contacts app; they just open the messaging app they want to use. That's the easiest way to chat.

What they're really arguing here is that Beeper is entitled to piggyback on the work Apple has done to make iMessage texting seamless between iOS and Mac users — and more so, that Beeper is entitled to free use of Apple's iMessage server and networking infrastructure.

Beeper is correctly arguing that Beeper Mini does not (and cannot) compromise the security of the iMessage protocol, but that doesn't mean that its existence doesn't open security concerns for Apple and its users.
If Apple can't control the clients permitted to connect to iMessage, it looks like they've lost control of the platform. And to some degree, they have lost control over the iMessage platform.
It's fun watching this cat-and-mouse game from the sidelines, but if I were Beeper, I wouldn't want to be playing.
key_insight: Beeper's real argument is that it deserves free access to Apple's iMessage infrastructure, but even if Beeper Mini doesn't break the protocol's security, it breaks Apple's end-to-end chain of trust and control over the platform.
